page: Vultr voc-s-24c-192gb-5760s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute server powered by an AMD EPYC x86_64 processor. It features 24 vCPUs derived from 12 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled, running at a base clock speed of 2.0 GHz. The system is equipped with 192.0 GB of RAM, providing a memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per vCPU. Local storage is bundled, consisting of a 5760 GB NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

In benchmark testing, the server demonstrates strong computational and memory subsystem performance. The stress-ng single-core and multi-core CPU evaluations place this instance in the top 25% of tested hardware. Memory bandwidth is particularly notable, with uncached large-block read and write speeds reaching 467.73 GB/sec and 282.99 GB/sec respectively, both scoring in the top 10% tier. Standard operations, such as gzip compression, bzip3 decompression, and Redis SET operations, perform in the average range, with Redis handling 51.19 million operations per second.

From a resource density perspective, the server offers high memory and storage capacity relative to its vCPU count, making it a qualitatively cost-efficient option for workloads requiring large datasets in memory. However, the shared CPU allocation represents a potential tradeoff for workloads requiring sustained, non-burstable maximum CPU performance. This configuration is highly suitable for memory-intensive databases, large-scale caching layers, data indexing, and general-purpose web serving applications that benefit from substantial local NVMe storage.
description: The Vultr voc-s-24c-192gb-5760s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute server configured with 24 shared vCPUs on an AMD EPYC x86_64 architecture, 192.0 GB of system memory, and a 5760 GB NVMe SSD. Operating at 2.0 GHz across 12 physical cores, the processor delivers strong multi-core performance, ranking in the top 25% in stress-ng benchmarks. Its memory subsystem is highly efficient, achieving top-tier uncached read bandwidth of 467.73 GB/sec. While the shared CPU allocation presents a tradeoff for sustained maximum compute workloads, the generous memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core and substantial local NVMe storage make this instance highly suitable for memory-intensive databases, caching layers, and large-scale web serving.

tagline: A memory-optimized virtual server featuring AMD EPYC processors, high-speed NVMe storage, and top-tier memory bandwidth for data-intensive workloads.
